The rain in Veridia always tasted like rust. It wasn’t a chemical thing, exactly, just… pervasive. And efficiency demanded a certain… purity. 

Kaelen adjusted the collar of his perfectly tailored, grey tunic. He was a Senior Strategist for the Directorate of Societal Optimization, and his job was to ensure Veridia remained, well, optimized.  He wasn’t a particularly ambitious man, just… thorough.  He’d spent the last decade meticulously crafting policies designed to maximize productivity and minimize disruption.  Today’s task was particularly delicate: Project Chimera.

“The data is undeniable, Director,” Kaelen said, his voice flat and precise. He gestured to a holographic projection displaying a graph charting a steady, alarming decline in workforce output. “Since the Implementation of the ‘Streamlining Initiative,’ we’ve seen a consistent and measurable decrease in overall productivity. The correlation is directly linked to the presence of individuals with disabilities.”

Director Varkos, a man whose face seemed permanently sculpted into a frown, nodded slowly. “The reports are clear.  Their… limitations, while perhaps individually regrettable, impede the collective.  We need to actively discourage their inclusion in the workforce.”

Kaelen steepled his fingers. “I’ve developed a comprehensive public campaign. It’s called ‘The Ascent.’ It’s designed to subtly, yet powerfully, shift societal perception.”

He began to detail the campaign, his voice a measured, almost clinical tone. “Phase One: ‘The Ripple Effect.’ We’ll begin with targeted advertisements – primarily visual, utilizing stylized, almost abstract imagery – depicting idealized, physically perfect workers.  These images will be consistently presented in contexts of success, innovation, and national pride. Think gleaming factories, soaring architectural designs, breakthroughs in energy production.  The key is to associate physical capability with achievement.  The ads will be subtly overlaid with a faint, shimmering effect – a visual ‘ripple’ – suggesting progress and upward movement.  We’ll use a calming, almost hypnotic soundtrack, designed to subtly reinforce this association.”

“Phase Two: ‘The Echo.’  We’ll introduce a series of short, professionally produced vignettes showcasing ‘successful’ individuals – all flawlessly able-bodied – achieving significant milestones. These vignettes will be aired during peak viewing hours, and will be heavily promoted through the Directorate’s network of information channels.  Each vignette will subtly imply that the individual’s success is *directly* attributable to their physical condition.  For example, a construction worker flawlessly erecting a skyscraper might be accompanied by a voiceover stating, ‘His unwavering dedication to his own physical form allowed him to overcome obstacles and achieve unparalleled heights.’  The visuals will be carefully calibrated to avoid explicit statements of prejudice, but the implication will be unmistakable.”

“Phase Three: ‘The Mirror.’ This is the most crucial phase. We’ll implement a system of ‘Performance Indicators’ – publicly displayed metrics measuring individual productivity. These indicators will be prominently featured in every workplace, public space, and even personal residences.  Individuals with disabilities will be assigned a lower ‘Performance Score.’  This score will be visible to everyone.  It will be subtly integrated into social interactions – influencing access to resources, opportunities, and even basic amenities.  Imagine a queuing system for public transport, where individuals with lower scores are automatically placed at the back.  Or a scholarship program, where the criteria prioritize demonstrable physical aptitude.  The system will be presented as a purely objective measure of value, designed to encourage competition and reward efficiency.”

“Finally, Phase Four: ‘The Legacy.’  We’ll launch a series of historical narratives – meticulously crafted documentaries and artistic representations – portraying the ‘decline’ of Veridia before the Streamlining Initiative. These narratives will depict a period of stagnation, inefficiency, and societal fragmentation, directly attributed to the presence of individuals with disabilities.  They will subtly suggest that Veridia’s current prosperity is a direct consequence of its commitment to eliminating such ‘impediments.’  The overarching message will be that progress is inextricably linked to physical perfection.”

Kaelen paused, looking at Director Varkos. “The campaign will be meticulously monitored. We’ll track shifts in public opinion, employment rates, and overall productivity.  The goal is not to overtly demonize individuals with disabilities, but to create an environment where their presence becomes… undesirable.  A quiet, pervasive sense of ‘rightness’ will emerge.  It’s about subtly shaping the collective consciousness.”

Varkos’s frown deepened. “Excellent.  It’s elegant.  And entirely necessary.  Veridia must remain optimized.”

Kaelen nodded, feeling a familiar, unsettling sense of satisfaction. He knew, with chilling certainty, that this campaign wouldn’t be about preventing suffering. It would be about ensuring a certain, sterile kind of progress.  As he turned to leave, he noticed a small, shimmering ripple on his own grey tunic, a subtle reminder of the ‘Ascent’ and the relentless pursuit of Veridia’s perfect efficiency.  The rain, of course, tasted like rust.
